jokes.text: Drew. I'm Drew now. / Oh, Drew. Sorry. / Apology not accepted. / Because it wasn't even necessary in the first place.
jokes.setup: Andy corrects his name and Pam apologizes
jokes.punchline: Andy rejects the apology because it wasn't needed - classic post-anger management overcorrection
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Andy's forced zen attitude. The misdirection of rejecting an apology because it wasn't needed is well-crafted.

jokes.text: new techniques for dealing with the grumpies
jokes.setup: Andy describing his anger management techniques
jokes.punchline: Calling his anger issues 'the grumpies' - infantile language for serious problems
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat showing Andy's therapeutic overcorrection with childish terminology.

jokes.text: No, I'm not gonna call you that. / Cool. / I can't control what you do. I can only control what I do.
jokes.setup: Jim flatly refuses to call Andy by his new name
jokes.punchline: Andy's forced zen response reveals how superficial his anger management transformation is
jokes.explanation: Great character dynamics - Jim's deadpan refusal contrasts perfectly with Andy's therapeutic mantras.

jokes.text: It's like slapping someone with silence.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining Amish shunning
jokes.punchline: The poetic yet ridiculous metaphor 'slapping someone with silence'
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Dwight - turning a simple concept into an overly dramatic metaphor. Highly quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: I was shunned from the age of four until my sixth birthday for not saving the excess oil from a can of tuna.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining his personal experience with shunning
jokes.punchline: Being shunned for two years as a small child over tuna oil
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight backstory - absurd childhood trauma over something trivial. The specificity of 'excess oil from a can of tuna' makes it memorable.

jokes.text: Andy, Dwight says welcome back and that he could use a hug. / Tell him that that's not true. / Dwight says that he actually doesn't know one single fact about bear attacks.
jokes.setup: Jim is supposed to relay messages during the shunning
jokes.punchline: Jim completely makes up increasingly ridiculous messages
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim prank - exploiting the shunning rule to mess with both Dwight and Andy. The bear attack reference is perfectly Dwight-specific.

jokes.text: Tell him that bears can climb faster than they can run. / Jim, tell him! / Andy... No, it's too far. / Damn you.
jokes.setup: Dwight desperately trying to correct misinformation about bears
jokes.punchline: Jim abandoning the bit because it's 'too far' and Dwight's frustrated 'Damn you'
jokes.explanation: Great payoff to the prank setup. Jim's casual 'too far' and Dwight's exasperation perfectly capture their dynamic.

jokes.text: if I know Darryl, it gonna be zoppity
jokes.setup: Michael describing Darryl's presentation
jokes.punchline: Michael's made-up word 'zoppity' trying to sound cool/urban
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael trying to appropriate slang incorrectly. 'Zoppity' became a fan favorite quote.

jokes.text: Pudge has driven the forklift. / Madge. / I thought your name was Pudge. / No. It's always been Madge.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to name people who have driven the forklift
jokes.punchline: He's been calling her the wrong name - she's always been Madge, not Pudge
jokes.explanation: Solid Michael cringe moment showing his poor attention to people. Simple but effective character beat.

jokes.text: Her? Yes, 'Her' is qualified to work a dangerous machine. You are not.
jokes.setup: Michael dismissively says 'Her' about Madge
jokes.punchline: Darryl sarcastically emphasizes 'Her' while putting Michael in his place
jokes.explanation: Good comeback from Darryl highlighting both Michael's disrespect and incompetence.

jokes.text: Hey, Darryl, how's it hanging?
jokes.setup: Darryl describing how he was injured
jokes.punchline: Michael's casual greeting while kicking the ladder was incredibly inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael moment - the casual greeting while causing serious injury. The pun aspect makes it even more inappropriate.

jokes.text: Bale 'er? I hardly know her.
jokes.setup: Discussion of people getting hurt by the baler
jokes.punchline: Michael's inappropriate 'hardly know her' joke during serious safety talk
jokes.explanation: Classic hack joke but perfectly deployed for Michael's character - inappropriate timing makes it funny in context.

jokes.text: Ever since March Madness ended, I am so bored.
jokes.setup: Michael wanting to bet on safety statistics
jokes.punchline: His motivation is pure boredom after basketball season
jokes.explanation: Good character insight - Michael's need for constant stimulation and gambling makes him treat serious safety concerns like entertainment.

jokes.text: It's a big red trash compactor. / It's not a trash compactor! / It's very dangerous, okay. / Don't disrespect a baler, Mike.
jokes.setup: Michael dismissively describing the baler
jokes.punchline: Darryl's increasingly frustrated corrections about respecting the machinery
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the culture clash - Michael's disrespect vs Darryl's serious approach to workplace safety.

jokes.text: Toby now has the floor and he is going to try not to screw this up like everything else in his life. / Let me rephrase that. / I believe that you can do safety training and make it sound just as good as Darryl.
jokes.setup: Michael introducing Toby for safety training
jokes.punchline: Brutal insult followed by fake supportive rephrase that's still backhanded
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael-Toby dynamic. The 'rephrase' that's equally insulting shows Michael's inability to be genuinely supportive.

jokes.text: That's like a half an hour, every hour? / Take them at the same time.
jokes.setup: Michael calculating break time recommendations
jokes.punchline: Toby's logical solution to take all breaks simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Solid logical humor - the absurdity of safety recommendations when calculated together.

jokes.text: Like sometimes computers can explode, can they not? / No. No.
jokes.setup: Michael asking about dangerous office situations
jokes.punchline: His ridiculous suggestion that computers explode
jokes.explanation: Good Michael moment showing his need to make everything dramatic and dangerous.

jokes.text: Long johns? / A shawl?
jokes.setup: Discussion of keeping warm in the office
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous clothing suggestions
jokes.explanation: Minor escalation gag with good character voices - Kevin's practical stupidity and Creed's randomness.

jokes.text: sedimentary lifestyle / Sedentary.
jokes.setup: Toby explaining office health risks
jokes.punchline: Toby mispronounces 'sedentary' as 'sedimentary'
jokes.explanation: Simple malapropism showing even Toby, the 'smart' one, makes mistakes. Oscar's correction is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: That's called having a fat butt, Michael. / No. No, it's sedentary. / Yeah, that's fat butt disease. / That's what you suffer from? / You have fat butt disease, Michael?
jokes.setup: Discussion of sedentary lifestyle health risks
jokes.punchline: Darryl reducing it to 'fat butt disease' and targeting Michael
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with Darryl's direct, mocking style contrasting with office-speak euphemisms.

jokes.text: Excuse me, sea monster, you weigh like a thousand pounds. / Yeah? Yeah. / I bet you'd like to swim with this sea monster, wouldn't you?
jokes.setup: Michael insulting Kelly after being called out
jokes.punchline: Kelly's confident, flirtatious comeback to the insult
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Kelly turning Michael's insult into flirtation shows her confidence and turns the tables.

jokes.text: You live a sweet little nerfy life sitting on your biscuit never having to risk it.
jokes.setup: Darryl describing office workers' soft lives
jokes.punchline: The rhythmic, almost poetic insult with 'nerfy' and 'sitting on your biscuit'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional writing - the rhythm and wordplay make this highly quotable. 'Sitting on your biscuit never having to risk it' became iconic.

jokes.text: What? So, Nerf isn't cool anymore?
jokes.setup: Being called nerfy by Darryl
jokes.punchline: Michael's confused reaction missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael beat - completely missing the insult and focusing on brand coolness instead.

jokes.text: I worked in a warehouse. Men's Wearhouse. I was a greeter.
jokes.setup: Michael claiming warehouse experience
jokes.punchline: It was retail, not industrial, and he was just a greeter
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - builds up to warehouse cred then deflates it completely. The specificity makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Hello, I'm Michael. Welcome to Men's Wearhouse. We have a special on khaki pants today.
jokes.setup: Michael demonstrating his greeter skills
jokes.punchline: His overly earnest, retail-specific greeting
jokes.explanation: Good followup showing Michael's pride in a mundane job. The specificity of khaki pants adds to the mundane nature.

jokes.text: He has spent hours up here at reception with you. Hours and hours. / Okay. Okay. / No, constantly. Like for years.
jokes.setup: Explaining why Jim won the jelly bean counting
jokes.punchline: Pam's increasingly defensive escalation about their time together
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Pam getting flustered about her relationship with Jim. The repetition builds the awkwardness.

jokes.text: Nobody commits suicide because they work with a baler.
jokes.setup: Michael comparing office depression to warehouse dangers
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific and dark statement about suicide and balers
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that's surprisingly logical in Michael's warped worldview. The specificity makes it memorable.

jokes.text: and your hair sticks up straight. And you know science.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining interactive museum exhibits
jokes.punchline: His childlike conclusion that touching the static ball equals 'knowing science'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael logic - reducing complex concepts to simple cause-and-effect. 'And you know science' is beautifully stupid.

jokes.text: Indubitably.
jokes.setup: Pam asking if Michael is okay
jokes.punchline: Michael's unnecessarily fancy response
jokes.explanation: Simple character beat showing Michael trying to sound sophisticated. The word choice is perfectly pretentious.

jokes.text: Depression. Wolves. / Depression.
jokes.setup: Michael listing office killers
jokes.punchline: Randomly adding 'wolves' to the list then correcting himself
jokes.explanation: Brilliant random Michael moment. The economy of 'Wolves' followed by immediate correction is perfect comedy timing.

jokes.text: A depression quilt. / No time to sew a quilt.
jokes.setup: Michael brainstorming visual aids for depression
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous concept then immediate practical rejection
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist Michael moment - the idea of a 'depression quilt' is wonderfully ridiculous.

jokes.text: Additionally, Pam, you win $10 because she said 'awesome' 12 times. And Jim, you win $5 because she mentioned six romantic comedies.
jokes.setup: Kelly explaining Netflix to Ryan
jokes.punchline: The office betting on Kelly's predictable speech patterns
jokes.explanation: Excellent running gag revealing the office has been betting on Kelly's verbal tics. Shows both her predictability and their boredom.

jokes.text: Seedless?
jokes.setup: Being told to buy watermelons for safety testing
jokes.punchline: Michael's focus on the wrong detail while planning something dangerous
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael moment - missing the gravity of the situation and focusing on trivial details.

jokes.text: Creed is eating an apple. / I found a potato.
jokes.setup: Observing Creed during the watermelon drop
jokes.punchline: Creed's non-sequitur about finding a potato
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed moment - random, inexplicable, delivered with complete seriousness.

jokes.text: If it's Stanley's, call the offices of James P. Albini, see if he handles hate crimes.
jokes.setup: A watermelon damaged someone's car
jokes.punchline: Michael's assumption that Stanley would pursue hate crime charges
jokes.explanation: Dark Michael humor showing his paranoia about racial issues. The specific lawyer name adds authenticity.

jokes.text: Have Padge do it or the sea monster.
jokes.setup: Michael assigning someone to use the baler
jokes.punchline: Still calling Madge 'Padge' and referencing Kelly as 'sea monster'
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Michael learned nothing from earlier corrections. Consistent character flaw.

jokes.text: I need you to acquire an inflatable house and/or castle. / You mean a Moonbounce. / What do you think? You've got an hour.
jokes.setup: Dwight giving Andy an urgent mission
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous formality around getting a bounce house
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction - Dwight's overly formal language for a bounce house and the time pressure.

jokes.text: Do you want a drawbridge? / Un-shun. / Yeah, that sounds good. Re-shun.
jokes.setup: Andy asking about bounce house features during shunning
jokes.punchline: Dwight briefly un-shunning to approve the drawbridge then immediately re-shunning
jokes.explanation: Excellent execution of the shunning gag. Dwight's dedication to the bit while still wanting the drawbridge is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: When you land, try and land like an eight-year-old. These bouncy castles are not designed for adults.
jokes.setup: Andy giving Michael safety advice for the bounce house
jokes.punchline: The absurd instruction to 'land like an eight-year-old'
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist advice. The specific age and practical concern make it funny.

jokes.text: Michael is awesome / Jumping off the roof / Bouncing on a bouncy thing / Show them who is boss / Rip a hole in the sun!
jokes.setup: Michael psyching himself up for the stunt
jokes.punchline: The escalation from practical to completely nonsensical with 'rip a hole in the sun'
jokes.explanation: Great escalation showing Michael's delusions of grandeur. 'Rip a hole in the sun' is memorably absurd.

jokes.text: Un-shun. I think he's suffering from depression. Re-shun.
jokes.setup: Someone asking about Michael's situation
jokes.punchline: Dwight's clinical assessment delivered within the shunning format
jokes.explanation: Perfect use of the shunning gag - Dwight maintaining the format while delivering serious information.

jokes.text: Un-shun. Never. Re-shun.
jokes.setup: Andy asking when the shunning will end
jokes.punchline: Dwight's definitive 'Never' delivered in shunning format
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to the shunning gag. The finality of 'Never' within the format is perfectly Dwight.

jokes.text: Will I be too warm in a long-sleeved tee? / Everyone's going to be fine in exactly what they're wearing! Let's go!
jokes.setup: People asking practical questions about going outside
jokes.punchline: Jim's increasingly frantic responses to trivial concerns while Michael is in crisis
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the office's misplaced priorities and Jim's exasperation.

jokes.text: Depression? Isn't that just a fancy word for feeling bummed out? / Dwight, you ignorant slut.
jokes.setup: Dwight minimizing depression
jokes.punchline: Michael's SNL reference response
jokes.explanation: Pop culture reference that works because it's perfectly deployed. Not original but earned through perfect timing and character.

jokes.text: If John Mellencamp ever wins an Oscar, I am going to be a very rich dude.
jokes.setup: Kevin taking 10,000 to 1 odds
jokes.punchline: The random, specific example of John Mellencamp winning an Oscar
jokes.explanation: Brilliant random Kevin moment. The specificity of John Mellencamp and Oscar makes it memorably absurd.

jokes.text: Dwight, you ignorant slut!
jokes.setup: Second performance of the roof scene
jokes.punchline: Michael repeating his SNL reference line
jokes.explanation: Callback that works in context but loses impact on repetition. Shows Michael's limited repertoire.

jokes.text: Well, you know, the first performance was a little off, but I really think they hit their stride in the second show. I might even bring my parents tomorrow to the matinee.
jokes.setup: Michael repeating his roof performance
jokes.punchline: Creed treating it like a theatrical production with multiple shows
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Creed moment - treating Michael's crisis like dinner theater. The specificity of bringing his parents to the matinee elevates it.

jokes.text: Hey, check it out, there's a castle over there. / Oh, my God, there is a castle. / No. There's nothing to see over there, people. There's nothing to see.
jokes.setup: The bounce house is visible
jokes.punchline: Everyone getting distracted by the bounce house while Michael is having his crisis
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag showing how the safety measure undermines Michael's dramatic moment.

jokes.text: They found the castle, Michael. / Damn it!
jokes.setup: The crowd noticing the bounce house
jokes.punchline: Michael's frustration that his safety measure ruined his dramatic moment
jokes.explanation: Good payoff showing Michael's plan backfiring. His frustration is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: He's going to kill himself pretending to kill himself.
jokes.setup: Michael's dangerous stunt meant to simulate danger
jokes.punchline: The ironic observation about actual danger in fake danger
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observation capturing the absurdity of the situation perfectly. The irony is both funny and concerning.

jokes.text: Hey, Michael, don't jump on the bouncy castle. You can't do that 'cause you're going to get horribly, horribly injured.
jokes.setup: Michael about to jump on the 'safety' measure
jokes.punchline: The bounce house being more dangerous than helpful
jokes.explanation: Good irony - the safety measure becoming the actual danger. Jim's concern is both genuine and funny.

jokes.text: A Repliee-Q-One-Expo female robot. They're only available in Japan. / Dwight, you are such a liar.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to figure out Pam's 'present' for Michael
jokes.punchline: His incredibly specific and bizarre guess about a Japanese robot
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight moment - the incredibly specific technical name suggests he's actually researched this. Quintessentially weird.

jokes.text: You told me that I lead a... A cushy, wimpy Nerf life. / Yeah. / But I never said you had nothing to live for.
jokes.setup: Michael referencing Darryl's earlier insult
jokes.punchline: Darryl's clarification that he never meant Michael should die
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Michael's tendency to catastrophize and Darryl's basic human decency.

jokes.text: The sex isn't nearly as good as it used to be.
jokes.setup: Michael talking about his relationship with Jan
jokes.punchline: Oversharing intimate details while on a roof in front of coworkers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael cringe - inappropriate oversharing at the worst possible moment. The specificity makes it uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Mike, you're a very brave man. I mean it takes courage just to be you. To get out of bed every single day, knowing full well you got to be you.
jokes.setup: Darryl trying to talk Michael down
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment that's actually more insulting than encouraging
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant writing. What sounds like encouragement is actually devastating. The structure builds to the crushing punchline perfectly. Became an iconic quote.

jokes.text: I couldn't do it. I ain't that strong and I ain't that brave. / I'm braver than you? / Way braver. / You Braveheart, man. / I Braveheart. I am.
jokes.setup: Darryl continuing his backhanded encouragement
jokes.punchline: Michael completely missing the insult and embracing the 'compliment'
jokes.explanation: Perfect followup showing Michael's delusions. His proud 'I Braveheart' while completely missing the point is beautiful.

jokes.text: Am I a hero? I really can't say, but yes.
jokes.setup: Michael reflecting on whether he's a hero
jokes.punchline: False modesty immediately undermined by self-aggrandizement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael ending - the misdirection from humility to ego is flawlessly executed. Became one of the most quoted Office lines.
